name: Large Magellanic Cloud
claimToFame: A satellite galaxy of the Milky Way, visible to the naked eye from the southern hemisphere. Home to thousands of well-studied Cepheid variables.
anomalyExplanation: Its motion is dominated by orbiting the Milky Way, not by the universe's expansion — so it doesn't sit on Hubble's law line.

name: Small Magellanic Cloud
claimToFame: The other naked-eye Magellanic galaxy. Henrietta Leavitt discovered the period–luminosity relation by studying Cepheids in this very galaxy.
anomalyExplanation: Like the LMC, the SMC is bound to the Milky Way — its velocity is gravitational, not cosmological.

name: Andromeda
claimToFame: The nearest large spiral galaxy, and the most distant thing visible to the naked eye. It's heading toward the Milky Way for a collision in ~4 billion years.
anomalyExplanation: Andromeda is blueshifted (moving toward us!) — its gravitational attraction with the Milky Way overwhelms cosmological expansion at this distance.

name: Triangulum
claimToFame: The third-largest galaxy in the Local Group. Slightly blueshifted because, like Andromeda, it's gravitationally bound to our neighbourhood.
anomalyExplanation: Local Group member — its motion is set by gravity within the group, not by Hubble flow.

name: IC 1613
claimToFame: A faint dwarf galaxy in the Local Group, used to calibrate the Cepheid distance scale because it's nearly free of dust.

name: Barnard's Galaxy
claimToFame: A Local Group dwarf irregular discovered by E. E. Barnard in 1884.

name: Bode's Galaxy
claimToFame: A grand-design spiral in Ursa Major, slightly blueshifted because the M81 group is falling toward the Local Group.
anomalyExplanation: Even though M81 is millions of parsecs away, local gravitational pull still beats Hubble flow.

name: Cigar Galaxy
claimToFame: A starburst galaxy. Tidal interactions with M81 have triggered a torrent of star formation in its core.

name: Centaurus A
claimToFame: A peculiar elliptical with a dramatic dust lane — the result of a recent merger with a smaller spiral. Hosts a supermassive black hole.

name: Whirlpool Galaxy
claimToFame: A face-on grand-design spiral interacting with its smaller companion NGC 5195. The first galaxy ever recognised as a spiral (Lord Rosse, 1845).

name: Sombrero Galaxy
claimToFame: Edge-on spiral with a vast halo and a striking dust lane. Bright bulge dominated by old stars.

name: M87
claimToFame: A giant elliptical at the heart of the Virgo Cluster. Its supermassive black hole was the first to be directly imaged (Event Horizon Telescope, 2019).

name: NGC 4258
claimToFame: A maser galaxy with a precisely measured geometric distance. The whole Cepheid distance ladder is anchored against this one number.

name: NGC 1015
claimToFame: A barred spiral and SH0ES Cepheid host. Its supernova SN 2009ig anchors a rung of the distance ladder.

name: NGC 1309
claimToFame: A face-on spiral that hosted the well-observed Type Ia supernova SN 2002fk.

name: NGC 1365
claimToFame: A textbook barred spiral with a strong active nucleus. SN 2012fr was a SH0ES calibrator here.

name: NGC 3370
claimToFame: A SH0ES Cepheid host with a supernova (SN 1994ae) used to calibrate Type Ia luminosities.

name: NGC 3627
claimToFame: A member of the Leo Triplet. SH0ES used Cepheids here together with SN 1989B.

name: Antennae Galaxies
claimToFame: A pair of colliding spirals throwing out long tidal tails. SN 2007sr provided a SH0ES rung here.

name: NGC 4424
claimToFame: A peculiar spiral disturbed by interactions in the Virgo Cluster. SH0ES Cepheid host (SN 2012cg).

name: NGC 4536
claimToFame: A starburst barred spiral on the outskirts of the Virgo Cluster (SH0ES host of SN 1981B).

name: NGC 4639
claimToFame: A Virgo Cluster spiral that hosted SN 1990N. One of the longest-baseline SH0ES Cepheid hosts.

name: NGC 5584
claimToFame: Riess+ 2011's flagship SH0ES galaxy — over 250 Cepheids identified by HST. SN 2007af.

name: NGC 7250
claimToFame: A peculiar barred spiral hosting SN 2013dy, used in the SH0ES distance ladder.

name: UGC 9391
claimToFame: One of the furthest galaxies where SH0ES could resolve Cepheids — the upper limit of the Cepheid rung of the ladder.

name: HDF-N 4-555.1
claimToFame: A spectroscopically-confirmed source from the original 1995 Hubble Deep Field North. At z ≈ 0.5 it's already showing relativistic effects.
anomalyExplanation: At this redshift the simple v = c·z formula starts to overestimate velocity — special relativity corrections matter.

name: HUDF z=3 galaxy
claimToFame: A high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy in the Hubble Ultra Deep Field. Its light has been travelling for over 11 billion years.
anomalyExplanation: Far beyond the regime where Hubble's law is linear — distance and velocity here come from a full cosmological model, not v = H₀·d.

name: NGC 7320
claimToFame: Appears to belong to Stephan's Quintet but its redshift is far smaller — it's a foreground galaxy projected onto the group by chance.
anomalyExplanation: Sky position can deceive! Other Quintet members sit at z ≈ 0.022 (nearly 10× further). NGC 7320 is just visually superimposed.

name: 3C 273
claimToFame: The first quasar ever identified (1963). Bright as a 13th-magnitude star, but hundreds of megaparsecs away — the closest we have to a portable demonstration of cosmological redshift.
anomalyExplanation: Bright doesn't mean nearby — quasars are powered by supermassive black holes and can outshine entire galaxies from billions of light-years away.

name: NGC 1068
claimToFame: The closest Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus. Strong emission lines make it an ideal practice target for redshift identification.
